question: [File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: Midnight.take (ERC777 hook reentrancy via collateral token)] Can an attacker exploit ERC777-like hooks in a collateral token to reenter Midnight during supplyCollateral under preconditions where the collateral token calls a hook on the sender during transferFrom, triggering the call sequence supplyCollateral(...) -> _position.collateral[collateralIndex] += assets -> safeTransferFrom(collateralToken, msg.sender, address(this), assets) -> token hook reenters supplyCollateral or withdrawCollateral, violating the invariant that collateral accounting must be consistent before and after external calls, causing scoped impact: attacker could double-count collateral or withdraw collateral that was not yet fully transferred? Proof idea: deploy ERC777-like collateral token with tokensToSend hook that reenters withdrawCollateral, assert health check catches the reentry attempt.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: Midnight.take + repay (fee-on-transfer + continuous fee accrual)] Can an attacker cause continuous fee accounting to desync under preconditions where the loan token is fee-on-transfer and credit is created via take, triggering the call sequence take(buyOffer, ...) -> buyerCreditIncrease computed on full units -> buyerPendingFeeIncrease = buyerCreditIncrease * continuousFee * timeToMaturity / WAD -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, address(this), buyerAssets - sellerAssets) where Midnight receives less, causing pendingFee to be computed on inflated credit while actual token balance is lower, violating the invariant that continuousFeeCredit must not exceed actual token balance, causing scoped impact: fee claimer can claim more continuous fee than the protocol holds, draining lender funds? Proof idea: create market with nonzero continuousFee, take with fee-on-transfer loan token, warp to maturity, updatePosition, assert continuousFeeCredit > actual balance attributable to fees.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: Midnight.liquidate (fee-on-transfer + bad debt realization)] Can an attacker cause bad debt accounting to desync under preconditions where the loan token is fee-on-transfer and a liquidation realizes bad debt, triggering the call sequence liquidate(...) -> badDebt computed from position.debt and collateral values -> _marketState.lossFactor updated based on totalUnits and badDebt -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, address(this), repaidUnits) where Midnight receives repaidUnits*(1-fee), causing lossFactor to be computed on the correct totalUnits but withdrawable to be inflated by the full repaidUnits, violating the invariant that lossFactor and withdrawable must be jointly consistent with actual token balance, causing scoped impact: lenders' credit is slashed by the correct lossFactor but they can withdraw more than the protocol holds? Proof idea: create undercollateralized position, liquidate with fee-on-transfer loan token, assert withdrawable > actual balance, then have lenders withdraw until revert.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: MidnightBundles.buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral (fee-on-transfer)] Can an attacker exploit the bundler's balance assumption with a fee-on-transfer loan token under preconditions where the loan token charges a fee on transferFrom, triggering the call sequence pullToken(loanToken, msg.sender, maxBuyerAssets, ...) -> bundler receives maxBuyerAssets*(1-fee); forceApproveMax; take(...) -> Midnight pulls full amounts from bundler; safeTransfer(loanToken, msg.sender, maxBuyerAssets - filledBuyerAssets - referralFeeAssets) -> bundler tries to return more than it holds, violating the invariant that the bundler must return exactly maxBuyerAssets - filledBuyerAssets - referralFeeAssets to msg.sender, causing scoped impact: the final safeTransfer reverts due to insufficient balance, freezing the entire bundler transaction and any collateral withdrawals within it? Proof idea: deploy fee-on-transfer loan token, call buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral, assert revert on final transfer; verify no partial state (collateral withdrawals) persists.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: MidnightBundles.supplyCollateralAndSellWithUnitsTarget (fee-on-transfer collateral)] Can an attacker exploit the bundler's collateral supply with a fee-on-transfer collateral token under preconditions where the collateral token charges a fee on transferFrom, triggering the call sequence pullToken(collateralToken, msg.sender, assets, ...) -> bundler receives assets*(1-fee); forceApproveMax; supplyCollateral(market, index, assets, taker) -> Midnight pulls assets from bundler but bundler only has assets*(1-fee), causing safeTransferFrom to revert or Midnight to record assets while only receiving assets*(1-fee), violating the invariant that collateral recorded must equal tokens received, causing scoped impact: either the bundler transaction reverts (DoS) or Midnight records inflated collateral enabling bad debt? Proof idea: deploy fee-on-transfer collateral, call supplyCollateralAndSellWithUnitsTarget, assert either revert or inflated collateral accounting.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: Midnight.take (callback returns non-CALLBACK_SUCCESS bytes32)] Can an attacker cause a take to revert by deploying a callback that returns a valid bytes32 that is not CALLBACK_SUCCESS under preconditions where the maker sets offer.callback to a contract whose onBuy returns keccak256('wrong'), triggering the call sequence take(...) -> IBuyCallback(buyerCallback).onBuy(...) returns keccak256('wrong') -> require(... == CALLBACK_SUCCESS, WrongBuyCallbackReturnValue()) reverts, violating the invariant that a maker-controlled callback must not be able to selectively block takers, causing scoped impact: maker can deploy a callback that returns wrong values only for specific takers or amounts, selectively freezing fills? Proof idea: deploy callback returning wrong bytes32 for taker == attacker, assert WrongBuyCallbackReturnValue revert; deploy callback returning CALLBACK_SUCCESS for all, assert success.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: Midnight.take (fee-on-transfer + settlement fee accounting)] Can an attacker cause settlement fee accounting to permanently exceed actual token balance under preconditions where the loan token is fee-on-transfer and many takes are executed, triggering the call sequence take(...) -> claimableSettlementFee[loanToken] += buyerAssets - sellerAssets (full amount) -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, address(this), buyerAssets - sellerAssets) -> Midnight receives (buyerAssets - sellerAssets)*(1-fee), causing claimableSettlementFee to accumulate faster than actual balance, violating the invariant that claimableSettlementFee[token] <= actual token balance attributable to fees, causing scoped impact: fee claimer can drain lender funds by claiming inflated settlement fees? Proof idea: execute N takes with fee-on-transfer loan token, assert claimableSettlementFee[token] > sum of actual fee tokens received, then claimSettlementFee(token, claimableSettlementFee[token], receiver) and assert revert or lender insolvency.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: Midnight.claimSettlementFee (fee-on-transfer)] Can the fee claimer trigger insolvency by claiming settlement fees with a fee-on-transfer loan token under preconditions where the loan token charges a fee on transfer, triggering the call sequence claimSettlementFee(token, amount, receiver) -> claimableSettlementFee[token] -= amount; safeTransfer(token, receiver, amount), causing the protocol to send amount but receiver only gets amount*(1-fee), while claimableSettlementFee is reduced by the full amount, violating the invariant that the protocol's token balance must cover all remaining claimable fees and lender withdrawals, causing scoped impact: each fee claim permanently reduces the protocol's balance by amount*fee, draining lender funds? Proof idea: claim settlement fees with fee-on-transfer token, assert receiver.balanceOf delta < amount and protocol balance delta == amount, repeat until lender withdrawal reverts.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: Midnight.claimContinuousFee (fee-on-transfer)] Can the fee claimer trigger insolvency by claiming continuous fees with a fee-on-transfer loan token under preconditions where the loan token charges a fee on transfer, triggering the call sequence claimContinuousFee(market, amount, receiver) -> continuousFeeCredit -= amount; totalUnits -= amount; withdrawable -= amount; safeTransfer(loanToken, receiver, amount), causing withdrawable to decrease by amount but receiver only gets amount*(1-fee), violating the invariant that withdrawable must equal actual withdrawable token balance, causing scoped impact: the protocol's balance decreases by amount but withdrawable decreases by amount, so the shortfall accumulates and lenders cannot fully withdraw? Proof idea: claim continuous fees with fee-on-transfer token, assert withdrawable decreased by amount but actual balance decreased by amount*(1-fee), then have lenders withdraw until revert.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: Midnight.take (fee-on-transfer + multicall)] Can an attacker amplify fee-on-transfer accounting errors via multicall under preconditions where the loan token is fee-on-transfer and multiple takes are batched in a single multicall, triggering the call sequence multicall([take1, take2, ..., takeN]) -> each take independently inflates claimableSettlementFee and withdrawable by the fee amount, causing N*fee total desync in a single transaction, violating the invariant that total token balance must cover all accounting entries, causing scoped impact: multicall amplifies the per-take accounting error, making insolvency reachable faster with fewer transactions? Proof idea: batch 10 takes in multicall with fee-on-transfer loan token, assert total accounting desync is 10x the single-take desync, compare to 10 sequential takes.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified (isRatified called by non-MIDNIGHT)] Can an attacker call isRatified directly to probe offer validity without triggering a take under preconditions where the attacker calls EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified directly (not through Midnight), triggering the call sequence isRatified(offer, ratifierData) -> require(msg.sender == MIDNIGHT, NotMidnight()) reverts, violating no invariant (this is correct behavior), but fuzz testing should confirm that the NotMidnight check cannot be bypassed via delegatecall or any other call path, causing scoped impact: if bypassable, attacker could probe offer validity or trigger side effects without going through Midnight's access controls? Proof idea: call isRatified from arbitrary address, assert NotMidnight revert; attempt via delegatecall from a contract, assert same revert.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: Midnight.take (fee-on-transfer + reduceOnly offer)] Can an attacker exploit fee-on-transfer behavior to bypass the reduceOnly constraint under preconditions where the loan token is fee-on-transfer and a reduceOnly buy offer is taken, triggering the call sequence take(reduceOnlyBuyOffer, ...) -> buyerCreditIncrease = zeroFloorSub(units, buyerPos.debt) -> require(!offer.reduceOnly || buyerCreditIncrease == 0, MakerCreditOrDebtIncreased()) -> if buyerCreditIncrease == 0 (debt reduction only), no credit increase occurs, but the fee-on-transfer means Midnight receives less than buyerAssets - sellerAssets, violating the invariant that a reduceOnly offer must not increase net protocol liability, causing scoped impact: the settlement fee accounting is inflated even for reduceOnly offers, contributing to insolvency? Proof idea: create reduceOnly buy offer, take with fee-on-transfer loan token, assert claimableSettlementFee inflated despite no credit increase.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: Midnight.take (fee-on-transfer + post-maturity)] Can an attacker exploit fee-on-transfer behavior post-maturity to inflate withdrawable beyond actual balance under preconditions where the loan token is fee-on-transfer and takes occur after maturity (sellerDebtIncrease == 0 enforced), triggering the call sequence take(buyOffer, ...) post-maturity -> sellerDebtIncrease == 0 enforced -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, address(this), buyerAssets - sellerAssets) -> Midnight receives less than accounted, causing claimableSettlementFee to be inflated even in post-maturity unwinding, violating the invariant that post-maturity settlement must not create new accounting deficits, causing scoped impact: post-maturity unwinding with fee-on-transfer token accelerates insolvency? Proof idea: warp past maturity, execute take with fee-on-transfer loan token, assert claimableSettlementFee inflated, compare to pre-maturity behavior.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: Midnight.supplyCollateral (fee-on-transfer + multi-collateral)] Can an attacker create a multi-collateral position with inflated collateral values using fee-on-transfer tokens under preconditions where multiple collateral tokens each charge a fee on transferFrom, triggering the call sequence supplyCollateral(market, 0, assets0, borrower) -> position.collateral[0] = assets0 (actual: assets0*(1-fee0)); supplyCollateral(market, 1, assets1, borrower) -> position.collateral[1] = assets1 (actual: assets1*(1-fee1)), causing maxDebt = sum(collateral[i] * price[i] * LLTV[i]) to be computed on inflated values, violating the invariant that maxDebt must not exceed the real collateral value at LLTV, causing scoped impact: borrower can take on more debt than their real collateral supports across all collateral types, creating amplified bad debt? Proof idea: fuzz N collateral tokens each with fee in [1, 500] bps, assert maxDebt computed by isHealthy exceeds real collateral value * LLTV.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: Midnight.take (callback revert + liquidation lock)] Can a malicious maker use a reverting callback to keep the liquidation lock active under preconditions where the seller's liquidation lock is set via tExchange before callbacks and the onSell callback reverts, triggering the call sequence take(...) -> tExchange(LIQUIDATION_LOCK_SLOT, id, seller, true) -> onSell reverts -> entire take reverts -> tExchange is rolled back (transient storage is reverted), violating the invariant that the liquidation lock must not persist across reverted transactions, causing scoped impact: if transient storage is NOT rolled back on revert, the seller's liquidation lock could be stuck, preventing liquidation of an unhealthy position? Proof idea: verify EIP-1153 transient storage is reverted on transaction revert; attempt take with reverting onSell, assert liquidationLocked(id, seller) == false after revert.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: Midnight.take (fee-on-transfer + group consumption)] Can an attacker exploit fee-on-transfer behavior to cause group consumption accounting to desync under preconditions where the loan token is fee-on-transfer and an assets-based offer (maxAssets > 0) is taken, triggering the call sequence take(...) -> newConsumed = consumed[offer.maker][offer.group] += buyerAssets (full amount) -> require(newConsumed <= offer.maxAssets) -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, address(this), buyerAssets - sellerAssets) -> Midnight receives less, causing consumed to track full buyerAssets while actual tokens received are less, violating the invariant that consumed amount must correspond to actual assets transferred, causing scoped impact: the offer appears more consumed than actual value transferred, potentially blocking future fills before the real economic limit is reached? Proof idea: create assets-based offer with maxAssets = 1000e18, take with fee-on-transfer token, assert consumed[maker][group] == buyerAssets but actual tokens received < buyerAssets.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified (Merkle second preimage attack)] Can an attacker forge a valid Merkle proof by exploiting the lack of domain separation between leaf and internal node hashes under preconditions where the attacker finds a bytes32 value that is simultaneously a valid offer hash and a valid internal node hash, triggering the call sequence isRatified(offer, abi.encode(sig, root, leafIndex, proof)) -> HashLib.isLeaf(root, hashOffer(offer), leafIndex, proof) -> hashNode uses keccak256(left || right) without domain separation, violating the invariant that only offers explicitly included in the signed Merkle tree can be ratified, causing scoped impact: attacker could ratify an offer not signed by the maker by constructing a proof where an internal node hash matches a crafted offer hash? Proof idea: attempt to find a collision between hashOffer output and hashNode output; verify computationally infeasible but confirm no structural shortcut exists in the tree construction.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: Midnight.take (fee-on-transfer + buyer is maker in buy offer)] Can an attacker exploit fee-on-transfer behavior when the buyer is the maker of a buy offer under preconditions where the loan token is fee-on-transfer and the maker's buy offer is taken by a taker, triggering the call sequence take(buyOffer, ...) -> buyer = offer.maker; payer = buyerCallback != address(0) ? buyerCallback : buyer -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, buyer, address(this), buyerAssets - sellerAssets) -> Midnight receives less than accounted, causing the maker's credit to increase by units while the protocol receives less than the settlement fee, violating the invariant that credit creation must be backed by actual token receipt, causing scoped impact: maker gains credit units without the protocol receiving full backing, contributing to insolvency? Proof idea: create buy offer with no callback, take with fee-on-transfer loan token, assert maker credit increased by units but protocol received less than buyerAssets - sellerAssets.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: Midnight.repay (fee-on-transfer + callback)] Can an attacker cause a double accounting error in repay with a fee-on-transfer loan token and a repay callback under preconditions where the loan token charges a fee and the callback is used to source funds, triggering the call sequence repay(market, units, onBehalf, callback, data) -> position.debt -= units; withdrawable += units; IRepayCallback(callback).onRepay(...) -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, callback, address(this), units) -> Midnight receives units*(1-fee), causing withdrawable to increase by units while only units*(1-fee) is received, violating the invariant that withdrawable must not exceed actual token balance, causing scoped impact: lenders can withdraw more than the protocol holds after fee-on-transfer repayments via callback? Proof idea: implement repay callback that sources fee-on-transfer tokens, repay N units, assert withdrawable increased by N but balance increased by N*(1-fee).

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: Midnight.take (false return on settlement fee transfer)] Can a loan token that returns false on transferFrom freeze all takes under preconditions where the loan token returns false specifically when the recipient is Midnight (for the settlement fee portion), triggering the call sequence take(...) -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, address(this), buyerAssets - sellerAssets) -> require(returndata.length == 0 || abi.decode(returndata, (bool)), TransferFromReturnedFalse()) reverts, violating the invariant that valid offers must be fillable, causing scoped impact: all takes in markets using this loan token are permanently frozen, locking all maker and taker positions? Proof idea: deploy ERC20 returning false when recipient == Midnight, create market, attempt take, assert TransferFromReturnedFalse revert; verify no state changes persist.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: Midnight.withdrawCollateral (false return on collateral transfer)] Can a collateral token that returns false on transfer freeze all collateral withdrawals under preconditions where the collateral token returns false on transfer, triggering the call sequence withdrawCollateral(market, collateralIndex, assets, onBehalf, receiver) -> _position.collateral[collateralIndex] -= assets; isHealthy check passes -> safeTransfer(collateralToken, receiver, assets) -> require(returndata.length == 0 || abi.decode(returndata, (bool)), TransferReturnedFalse()) reverts, violating the invariant that borrowers must always be able to withdraw healthy collateral, causing scoped impact: all collateral withdrawals for this token are permanently frozen, trapping borrower collateral in the protocol? Proof idea: deploy ERC20 returning false on transfer, create market with it as collateral, supply collateral, attempt withdrawCollateral, assert TransferReturnedFalse revert.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: Midnight.take (fee-on-transfer + totalUnits accounting)] Can an attacker cause totalUnits to diverge from actual token balance under preconditions where the loan token is fee-on-transfer and credit is created via take, triggering the call sequence take(buyOffer, ...) -> _marketState.totalUnits += buyerCreditIncrease (full units) -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, address(this), buyerAssets - sellerAssets) -> Midnight receives less, causing totalUnits to grow faster than actual token balance, violating the invariant that totalUnits must not exceed actual token balance minus collateral and fees, causing scoped impact: the lossFactor computation in liquidate uses totalUnits, so inflated totalUnits causes bad debt to be under-socialized, leaving lenders with more credit than the protocol can honor? Proof idea: create market, take with fee-on-transfer loan token, assert totalUnits > actual balance, then realize bad debt and assert lossFactor is computed on inflated totalUnits.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: Midnight.take (callback revert + consumed state)] Can a reverting callback cause consumed state to be rolled back, enabling offer replay under preconditions where the onBuy callback reverts after consumed is updated, triggering the call sequence take(...) -> consumed[maker][group] += units -> IBuyCallback(buyerCallback).onBuy(...) reverts -> entire take reverts -> consumed[maker][group] is rolled back to original value, violating the invariant that consumed state must be monotonically increasing, causing scoped impact: if consumed rollback is expected (correct Solidity behavior), verify that no path allows consumed to be updated without the corresponding token transfer, preventing replay? Proof idea: attempt take with reverting callback, assert consumed[maker][group] unchanged after revert; then attempt take with non-reverting callback, assert consumed updated.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: Midnight.flashLoan (false return on repayment)] Can a flash loan callback that returns false on the repayment transferFrom freeze the flash loan mechanism under preconditions where the flash loan token returns false on transferFrom when called by the callback to repay, triggering the call sequence flashLoan([token], [assets], callback, data) -> safeTransfer(token, callback, assets) -> onFlashLoan returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS -> safeTransferFrom(token, callback, address(this), assets) -> require(returndata.length == 0 || abi.decode(returndata, (bool)), TransferFromReturnedFalse()) reverts, violating the invariant that flash loans must be repayable, causing scoped impact: flash loans with this token are permanently frozen, blocking any protocol integrations that rely on flash loans? Proof idea: deploy ERC20 returning false on transferFrom from callback address, attempt flashLoan, assert TransferFromReturnedFalse revert.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/interfaces/IEcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: Midnight.take (fee-on-transfer + sellerAssets to receiver)] Can an attacker cause the seller to receive fewer assets than their debt obligation under preconditions where the loan token is fee-on-transfer and the seller is a taker of a buy offer, triggering the call sequence take(buyOffer, ...) -> sellerAssets = units.mulDivUp(sellerPrice, WAD) -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, receiver, sellerAssets) -> receiver gets sellerAssets*(1-fee), causing the seller's debt to increase by units at full value while they receive sellerAssets*(1-fee), violating the invariant that a seller's received assets must equal their debt obligation at the agreed price, causing scoped impact: seller is systematically underpaid relative to their debt, creating a loss on every borrow with fee-on-transfer loan tokens? Proof idea: fuzz fee in [1, 1000] bps and sellerPrice, assert receiver.balanceOf delta == sellerAssets*(1-fee) but seller.debt delta == units.
